metadata.title: WHO Guidelines on Clinical Evaluation of Vaccines: Regulatory Expectations (Annex 1)
metadata.document_version: WHO Technical Report Series No. 924, Annex 1
metadata.note: File named TRS1004_Annex9 but document is TRS 924, Annex 1 (2004). Content covers full clinical evaluation regulatory expectations.

rules.rule_text: Preclinical evaluation of a vaccine is a prerequisite for the initiation of clinical trials; laboratory evaluation should continue throughout both preclinical and clinical phases of vaccine development.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.1 General remarks
rules.verbatim_quote: The preclinical evaluation of a vaccine is a prerequisite for the initiation of clinical trials. Laboratory evaluation should however be continued throughout both the preclinical and clinical phases of vaccine development.

rules.rule_text: When preclinical testing is performed in animals, there must always be a clear rationale for doing so, and the study must be performed in compliance with Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) guidelines and national guidelines on animal experimentation.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.1 General remarks
rules.verbatim_quote: When preclinical testing is performed in animals, there should always be a clear rationale for doing so, and the study should be performed in compliance with Good laboratory practice guidelines (11) and with national guidelines on animal experimentation.

rules.rule_text: Product characterization must be completed by the beginning of the Phase III stage of development; the final manufacturing process, specifications, and batch release testing procedures must be established before Phase III.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.2 Production, characterization and quality assurance
rules.verbatim_quote: The characterization, standardization and control of the components, safety and potency of vaccine preparations are key issues during development... product characterization should be completed by the beginning of the phase III stage of development.
cross_references: WHO GMP for biological products TRS 822 Annex 1

rules.rule_text: A batch release protocol providing an outline of production and a summary of test results and establishment specifications must be available for each batch of candidate vaccine used in clinical trials.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.2 Production, characterization and quality assurance
rules.verbatim_quote: A batch release protocol providing an outline of production and a summary of the test results and establishment specifications should be available for each batch.

rules.rule_text: Candidate vaccines for clinical trials must be prepared according to WHO Good Manufacturing Practices for pharmaceutical and biological products; Standard Operating Procedures covering all aspects of production, quality control, storage, and distribution must be documented.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.2 Production, characterization and quality assurance
rules.verbatim_quote: Candidate vaccines for clinical trials should be prepared according to good manufacturing practices. The general manufacturing recommendations contained in good manufacturing practices for pharmaceutical and biological products (3–5) should be applied by all establishments involved in producing candidate vaccine for clinical studies. Standard operating procedures covering all aspects of production, quality control, storage and distribution should be documented.
cross_references: WHO TRS 823 Annex 1 (GMP for pharmaceutical products)
WHO TRS 822 Annex 1 (GMP for biological products)

rules.rule_text: Sufficient samples of each batch of candidate vaccine, together with a record of analyses and characteristics, must be kept for future reference by the manufacturer and ideally a national control laboratory (NCL) for at least the duration of the anticipated or approved shelf-life and preferably longer.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.2 Production, characterization and quality assurance
rules.verbatim_quote: In accordance with good clinical practice, sufficient samples of each batch of candidate vaccine, together with a record of analyses and characteristics, must be kept for future reference by the manufacturer and ideally a national control laboratory (NCL) for possible subsequent re-testing and investigation. The product should be stored under safe and stable conditions for at least the duration of its anticipated or approved shelf-life and preferably longer.

rules.rule_text: Long-term, real-time stability studies under real conditions of use must support the expiry date of the product for licensure; accelerated stability data may support preliminary data only.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.2 Production, characterization and quality assurance
rules.verbatim_quote: Further data on stability to support the expiry date of the product for licence should be based on long-term, real-time, stability studies under the real conditions of use. All relevant documentation should be made available to the regulatory authorities.
rules.exceptions: Accelerated stability data may be used to support preliminary data generated at the normal storage temperature.

rules.rule_text: Adjuvants used in vaccines must comply with pharmacopoeial requirements where they exist and must not cause unacceptable reactogenicity; compatibility of the adjuvant(s) with all antigenic components must be demonstrated on a lot-to-lot basis.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.5.1 Adjuvants
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important that the adjuvants used comply with pharmacopoeial requirements where they exist, and that they do not cause unacceptable reactogenicity. Compatibility of the adjuvant(s) with all the antigenic components of the vaccine should be demonstrated. Where relevant, adsorption of all the antigenic components present in the vaccine, should be shown to be consistent on a lot-to-lot basis.

rules.rule_text: For new adjuvants, appropriate preclinical studies are required before clinical use; if no toxicological data exist for a new adjuvant, toxicity studies of the adjuvant alone must be performed first, followed by preclinical studies of the adjuvant-antigen combination.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.5.1 Adjuvants
rules.verbatim_quote: If a new adjuvant is proposed for use in a vaccine formulation, appropriate preclinical studies are necessary... If no toxicological data exist for a new adjuvant, toxicity studies of the adjuvant alone should first be performed. Preclinical animal studies to determine the safety profile of the combination of adjuvant and vaccine should also be undertaken.
rules.exceptions: Not required for well-established adjuvants (e.g., aluminium salts) with documented safety profiles.

rules.rule_text: For live attenuated vaccines, markers of attenuation (genetic sequences) must be identified and used in clinical trials to monitor excretion studies and genetic stability during clinical evaluation, phase by phase.
rules.source_section: Part A. A.5.3.5 Live attenuated vaccines
rules.verbatim_quote: The major concern related to live attenuated vaccines is potential reversion to virulence and the possible transmissibility and exchange of genetic information with wild type or other microorganisms. Every effort should be made to identify markers of attenuation (genetic sequences) which should be used in clinical trials to monitor the results of excretion studies and during clinical evaluation, phase by phase.

rules.rule_text: All clinical trials must adhere to WHO Good Clinical Practice standards; the general principles of WHO GCP guidelines for trials on pharmaceutical products also apply to vaccine studies.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.1 General remarks
rules.verbatim_quote: All clinical trials should adhere to the standards described for good clinical practice. The general principles of the WHO guidelines for good clinical practice already in place for trials of pharmaceutical products, also apply to vaccine studies.
rules.exceptions: None; vaccines demand special consideration due to administration in healthy individuals and biological complexity.

rules.rule_text: In formal regulatory frameworks, regulatory approval (e.g., IND in the US, Clinical Trial Certificate or Clinical Trial Exemption in the UK) is required before initiating any phase of clinical trials; ethical clearance from an independent ethics committee is required in all countries.
rules.source_section: Regulation of vaccines — Phases of clinical development
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical trials in humans are classified into three phases: phase I, phase II and phase III and in certain countries formal regulatory approval is required to undertake any of these studies... This is in addition to ethical clearance which is required for clinical trials in all countries. All studies of human subjects require proper ethical review, in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.
rules.exceptions: Regulatory approval form varies by country (IND, CTC, CTX, etc.).

rules.rule_text: Review by an independent ethics committee, functioning in accordance with GCP standards, is mandatory before the start of any vaccine trial; no subject may be included in a clinical trial without proper informed consent in writing; informed consent for children must be obtained from their parent or guardian.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.4 Ethical considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: For any study, a review by an independent ethics committee, functioning in accordance with good clinical practice standards, is mandatory... No subject may be included in a clinical trial without proper informed consent in writing. Informed consent for children should be obtained from their parent or guardian.

rules.rule_text: Specific inclusion and exclusion criteria must be defined for each phase of a clinical trial in advance; subjects enrolled must be in the required age group, resident in the study area, examined by the study physician, and able to give signed informed consent. Previous exposure to vaccines and antigens must be recorded for all participants.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.1.1 Inclusion and exclusion criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Specific inclusion and exclusion criteria should be defined for each phase of a trial. The subjects enrolled in the trial should be in the required age group, resident within the defined study area(s) during selection, examined by the study physician and able to give their signed informed consent... Previous exposure to vaccines and antigens should be recorded for all participants.

rules.rule_text: Contraindications to subsequent doses must be defined in the protocol; these include serious reactions after a prior dose (e.g., neurological reaction, fever ≥40°C within 48 hours, generalized allergic reaction within 48 hours).
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.1.1 Inclusion and exclusion criteria
rules.verbatim_quote: Criteria should also be established for contraindications to the administration of a subsequent (second or third) dose of vaccine, if applicable. These might include serious reaction after the first or second dose (e.g. neurological reaction), fever greater than or equal to 40°C within 48 hours of administration or a generalized allergic reaction within 48 hours of administration.

rules.rule_text: Case definitions for trial endpoints must be clearly defined at the outset and before any efficacy study commences; they must be justified, described in the study protocol, and applied consistently throughout the duration of the study at all study sites.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.6 Case detection, case ascertainment and case definition
rules.verbatim_quote: It is essential that the case definitions for the trial end-points be clearly defined at the outset. Case definitions and methods of case detection should be justified and described in the study protocol... Defined and validated methods should be applied consistently for the duration of the study, at all study sites.

rules.rule_text: All serious adverse events must be reported by investigators to the sponsor immediately unless the protocol specifies otherwise; investigators must also comply with reporting requirements for unexpected serious adverse reactions to the NRA and the independent ethics committee.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.7 Monitoring and reporting adverse events
rules.verbatim_quote: Investigators should report all serious adverse events to the sponsor immediately unless they are identified by the protocol as not needing to be reported immediately. Investigators should also comply with the applicable regulatory requirements related to the reporting of unexpected serious adverse reactions to the NRA and the independent ethics committee.
rules.exceptions: Events designated by the protocol as not requiring immediate reporting are excepted.

rules.rule_text: After completion or termination of a trial, all recorded adverse events must be listed, evaluated and discussed in the final report; reporting of adverse events must be part of the protocol design.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.7 Monitoring and reporting adverse events
rules.verbatim_quote: After the trial has been completed or terminated, all recorded adverse events should be listed, evaluated and discussed in the final report. Reporting of adverse events should be part of the protocol design.

rules.rule_text: Data Safety Monitoring Boards (DSMBs) must be in place for large-scale Phase II and Phase III trials and post-marketing surveillance studies; DSMBs must be independent and preferably linked to the independent ethics committee.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.2.7 Monitoring and reporting adverse events
rules.verbatim_quote: For some trials, such as large-scale phase II and phase III trials and post-marketing surveillance studies, data safety monitoring boards (DSMBs) need to be in place, to ensure adequate safety monitoring... DSMBs must be independent and preferably linked to the independent ethics committee.
rules.exceptions: DSMBs may also be required for Phase I in special cases.

rules.rule_text: Statistical analysis must describe: (a) procedures for randomization and blinding; (b) primary and secondary objectives; (c) outcome variables, null and alternative hypotheses, significance level, anticipated power, and statistical methods for each endpoint; (d) intent-to-treat estimates alongside per-protocol estimates; and (e) confidence intervals for all statistical estimates.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.1 General principles
rules.verbatim_quote: The procedures for randomization and blinding should be described in the study protocol... The protocol should state explicitly the outcome variables to be analysed, the null and alternative hypothesis to be tested, the significance level the anticipated power and the statistical methods to be used for assessing each end-point... For the evaluation of efficacy, intent-to-treat estimates should accompany traditional per-protocol estimation... Statistical estimates should include confidence intervals.

rules.rule_text: The gold standard Phase III efficacy trial design is the prospective randomized double-blind controlled trial of protective efficacy; this design must be used unless fully justified deviation is provided. Non-randomized approaches should be avoided in Phase III trials.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.7.1.1 Trial design / B.7.1.2 Randomized double-blind controlled trials
rules.verbatim_quote: The gold standard for assessing the prevention of disease or infection in a phase III trial is the prospective randomized double-blind controlled trial of protective efficacy... The most effective efficacy trials are double-blind, randomized and controlled... Every effort should be made to use randomized well-controlled designs for phase III trials.
rules.exceptions: Alternative designs allowed only when fully justified; observational studies are usually Phase IV post-licensure.

rules.rule_text: For non-inferiority (one-sided equivalence) trials based on immune response, the trial must be designed to show: (a) the proportion of responders in the new vaccine group is not less than in the control group by more than a pre-specified margin (often 0.10); or (b) the ratio of geometric mean titres (or concentrations) of the new vaccine to the control is not less than a pre-specified ratio (e.g., 0.50 or 0.67).
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.3.2 Non-inferiority trials
rules.verbatim_quote: A non-inferiority trial of vaccine efficacy is generally designed to show that the use of a new vaccine gives a relative risk, relative incidence rate or relative hazard rate of a disease... is not greater than a pre-specified clinically relevant quantity. In a non-inferiority trial based on immune response, the relative effect of interest may be a difference in proportions of subjects responding in a pre-specified manner, or a ratio of geometric mean titres or concentrations. For the former, the trial is designed to show that the proportion of subjects responding to the new vaccine is not less than the proportion of subjects responding in the control group by as much as a pre-specified quantity (often 0.10). For the evaluation of titres, the trial may be designed to demonstrate that the ratio of the geometric mean titre (or concentration) of the new vaccine relative to the control is not less than some pre-specified ratio (e.g. 0.50 or 0.67).
rules.exceptions: Non-inferiority margins must be scientifically justified based on clinical, laboratory, and statistical judgment.

rules.rule_text: For lot consistency evaluation (two-sided equivalence trials), the lots are considered equivalent when a two-sided confidence interval for the appropriate relative effect (ratio of geometric mean antibody concentrations or relative risk of adverse event) falls entirely within pre-specified limits.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.3.3 Two-sided equivalence trials
rules.verbatim_quote: The lots are considered equivalent, or consistently manufactured, when a two-sided confidence interval for the appropriate relative effect (e.g. ratio of geometric mean antibody concentrations or relative risk of adverse event) falls entirely within pre-specified limits. The choice of the equivalence margins should be scientifically justified.

rules.rule_text: Sample size calculations must be clearly explained in the protocol for each primary endpoint (immunogenicity, safety, and efficacy); the largest estimate determines the number of subjects to be enrolled.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.4 Sample size
rules.verbatim_quote: The protocol should clearly explain calculations of sample size required for each primary end-point (immunogenicity, safety and efficacy) and the largest estimate should determine the number of subjects to be enrolled.

rules.rule_text: For evaluation of common local reactogenicity, approximately 300 subjects are needed per comparison group; for adequate pre-licensure safety assurance in randomized controlled settings, enrolment of more than 5000 subjects may be appropriate.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.4.4 Sample size in safety evaluations
rules.verbatim_quote: For evaluation of common local reactogenicity, approximately 300 subjects are needed for each comparison group. However, depending on the type of vaccine, the disease indication, and the target population, enrolment of more than 5000 subjects may be appropriate to provide reasonable assurance of safety pre-licensure in randomized, controlled settings.
rules.exceptions: Numbers based on one-sided confidence interval when no adverse events observed; increase if adverse events observed.

rules.rule_text: In most confirmatory Phase III clinical trials, a follow-up period of at least 6 months subsequent to the last vaccination is required; for vaccines intended for immunization programmes, subjects must be followed for at least 1 year following the last vaccination.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.3.5 Duration of study
rules.verbatim_quote: In most confirmatory clinical trials this implies a follow-up period of at least 6 months subsequent to the last vaccination... For vaccines intended for use in immunization programmes, subjects should be followed up for at least 1 year following the last vaccination to obtain serological and clinical information on the persistence of protection and the possible need for a booster vaccination.
rules.exceptions: Duration depends on outcome measurement (clinical endpoint, immunogenicity, safety), vaccination strategy, and novelty/type of vaccine.

rules.rule_text: Phase I studies must be conducted in research environments with adequate laboratory support and very carefully monitored; the primary focus is determination of clinical tolerance and safety.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.5 Phase I studies
rules.verbatim_quote: All phase I studies should be conducted in research environments with adequate laboratory support and very carefully monitored. Phase I studies are usually-open label studies and are not randomized with placebo control groups... Phase I studies are small-scale studies of which the primary focus is the determination of clinical tolerance and safety.

rules.rule_text: Phase II studies must define the optimal dose, initial schedule, and safety profile of the candidate vaccine before Phase III trials can begin; Phase II studies are often randomized and well-controlled.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.6 Phase II studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Ultimately, the phase II studies should define the optimal dose, initial schedule and safety profile of a candidate vaccine before the phase III trials can begin.

rules.rule_text: Immunological data from Phase II trials must include geometric mean titre, median, standard deviation, and the range of antibodies in pre- and post-vaccination sera; each assay must be fully documented and consistent use of a validated assay is essential.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.6 Phase II studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Immunological data from phase II trials should be documented, including geometric mean titre, median, standard deviation, and the range of antibodies in pre and post-vaccination sera... Each assay should be fully documented and consistent use of a validated assay is essential.
rules.exceptions: When standardized assay methodologies are available, they should be used (WHO recommendations, European Pharmacopoeia, FDA).

rules.rule_text: All reported serious adverse events in Phase III trials must be described in detail with the following information recorded: patient identification, study identification, type of event, time after vaccination, patient characteristics/concomitant medications, actions taken, and course/duration/outcome/investigator's causality assessment.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.7.5 Serious adverse events
rules.verbatim_quote: All reported serious adverse events should be described in detail and the following information recorded: patient's study number or identification number; study identification; type of adverse event; how long after the vaccination the adverse event occurred; patient characteristics, including any underlying diseases, concomitant vaccinations or drugs; actions taken, e.g. therapy administered; and course of the adverse event including duration, outcome and investigator's assessment of causality.

rules.rule_text: Safety evaluation in Phase III trials must include all subjects enrolled in all trials who receive at least one dose of vaccine; safety surveillance must begin from the start of enrolment. Safety issues identified during preclinical testing must be specifically addressed in Phase I, II, and III clinical trials.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.7.4 Safety evaluations in phase III trials
rules.verbatim_quote: The safety evaluation should include all subjects enrolled in all trials who receive at least one dose of vaccine, and safety surveillance should begin from the start of enrolment... Safety issues identified during preclinical testing should be specifically addressed in the phase I, II and III clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Immune responses must always be evaluated as part of Phase III clinical protection studies; validated and standardized assays are essential for immunological correlates of protection to be clinically meaningful.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.7.2.3 Correlates of protection
rules.verbatim_quote: Immune responses should always be evaluated as part of a phase III clinical protection study with the aim of identifying immunological correlates of protection. For such an evaluation to be clinically meaningful, validated standardized assays are essential.

rules.rule_text: Bridging studies for manufacturing changes must ordinarily be randomized controlled trials that demonstrate comparability of the new product with the previous version; any proposed change in vaccine production must be shown by the manufacturer to result in a product equivalent to that used in preclinical or earlier clinical testing.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.8.1 Design and extent / B.8.2.1 Bridging for manufacturing changes
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical bridging studies (to support comparability with respect to the manufacturing process, change in product composition, or a new dose, route or schedule for immunization) should ordinarily be randomized controlled trials... Any proposed change in the production of a vaccine must be shown by the manufacturer to result in a product equivalent to that used in preclinical (or earlier clinical) testing.
rules.exceptions: Clinical bridging not required when clearly demonstrated by pharmaceutical/preclinical data that the change will not alter clinical efficacy or safety.

rules.rule_text: A safety bridging study for the target population is required when: there are index cases of serious adverse events in foreign clinical data; there are differences in adverse event reporting; insufficient safety data on target population are available; the safety profile cannot be extrapolated from foreign data; or immunization schedules/antigenic doses differ from foreign trials.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.8.2.4 Bridging studies for safety
rules.verbatim_quote: A special safety study is required if an efficacy bridging study is not needed, or when the efficacy study does not provide adequate safety information, including when: there is an index case (the individual in whom the event was first reported) or cases of a serious adverse event in foreign clinical data (generated outside the target region); there are differences in reporting of adverse events elsewhere; insufficient data on safety in the target population are available from an efficacy bridging study; the safety profile cannot be extrapolated from foreign data to the target population; or immunization schedules and/or antigenic doses differ from those used in foreign trials.
rules.exceptions: A limited safety study may precede the clinical bridging study to ensure serious adverse events do not occur at a high rate.

rules.rule_text: Marketing authorization holders must commit to presenting a postmarketing surveillance programme at licensure; all national regulatory authorities should put in place a system for pharmacovigilance for vaccines. Outcomes of surveillance must be reported to national authorities and/or the marketing authorization holder and published.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.9 Post-licensure studies and surveillance
rules.verbatim_quote: To ensure adequate postmarketing surveillance marketing authorization holders should be committed to presenting a postmarketing surveillance programme at licensure and all national regulatory authorities should endeavour to put in place a system for pharmacovigilance for vaccines. The outcomes of surveillance (assessments of effectiveness, adverse events and quality) should be reported to the national authorities and/or the marketing authorization holder, and they should be published.

rules.rule_text: A postmarketing oversight policy must be established by the national regulatory authority to enable control of product release, periodic inspections, reporting mechanisms, recall of batches, revoking marketing approvals, approval of manufacturing changes, and evaluation of new indications/dose regimens.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.9.4 Monitoring of postmarketing surveillance
rules.verbatim_quote: A postmarketing oversight policy should be established by a national regulatory authority to enable control of product release, periodic inspections, reporting mechanisms, recall of batches, or, if necessary, for revoking marketing approvals, approval of manufacturing changes, and evaluation and approval of new indications and/or dose regimens.

rules.rule_text: A serosurveillance protocol must be presented at the time of marketing authorization or implementation of a vaccination programme; the structured plan must include information on participating institutes and intervals of reporting (usually every 6 months, for 5 years).
rules.source_section: Part B. B.9.2 Evaluation of vaccine effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: A protocol for serosurveillance should be presented at the time of marketing authorization, or implementation of a vaccination programme. A structured plan for executing the programme should be presented, including information on participating institute(s) and intervals of reporting (usually every 6 months, for 5 years).

rules.rule_text: For combination vaccines, the immunogenicity and safety of the new combination must be compared with the effects of simultaneous but separate administration of the individual vaccines; each individual component is expected to add materially to the prophylactic effect.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.10 Special considerations for combination vaccines / B.10.1 Efficacy studies
rules.verbatim_quote: The immunogenicity and safety of a new combination should be compared with the effects of simultaneous, but separate, administration of the individual vaccines... Each of the individual components is expected to add materially to the prophylactic effect of the vaccine.
rules.exceptions: If serological correlates of protection are validated for each component, immunogenicity endpoints rather than clinical protection endpoints may be used.

rules.rule_text: For monovalent vaccines intended for simultaneous administration with other vaccines, any clinically relevant interference must be ruled out; immunological interference and adverse safety interactions after simultaneous administration must be compared with results of separate administration.
rules.source_section: Part B. B.10.2 Safety analysis / Simultaneous administration
rules.verbatim_quote: For monovalent vaccines intended for simultaneous administration with other vaccines to the target population, any clinically relevant interference with the other vaccines should be ruled out. Immunological interference and adverse safety interactions after simultaneous administration should be compared with the results of separate administration of the (new) vaccine component(s) at different times.

rules.rule_text: Regulatory issues related to a vaccine must be considered early in the development process; dialogue with the appropriate national regulatory authority must be established early, and the NRA must review development plans and clarify requirements for clinical trials as well as for marketing approval.
rules.source_section: Regulation of vaccines
rules.verbatim_quote: Regulatory issues related to a particular candidate vaccine should be considered early in the development process, since compliance with regulatory requirements is the basis for eventual approval. It is strongly recommended that dialogue with the appropriate national regulatory authority be established early on. The national regulatory authority should review the plans for development of the candidate vaccine and clarify requirements for carrying out clinical trials, as well as for marketing approval.
